coredata.dc:title: Coordination-induced activation of reversible Co(II)/Co(III) redox reaction in carbon nanodots/cobalt hexacyanoferrate composites with enhanced electrochemical performance
coredata.dc:creator: Song, Zhaoxia
Liu, Wei
Zhou, Quan
Du, Jiaqi
Chen, Junlin
Wei, Xiaofei
Hua, Ruinian
Zhao, Zhongfu
coredata.dc:description: Cobalt hexacyanoferrate (CoHCF) as a promising cathode material of asymmetrical supercapacitors generally undergoes a single redox reaction of Fe(II)/Fe(III) couple to store energy in neutral aqueous electrolytes. Its framework Co ions are electrochemically inactivated, leading to a limited electrochemical capacity. Herein, we develop a strategy to activate the reversible Co(II)/Co(III) redox reaction through tuning the microenvironment of Co ions. A carbon nanodots/CoHCF (C-dots/CoHCF) hybrid nanostructure has been successfully fabricated via a simple co-precipitation reaction of Co2+ and [Fe(CN)6]3- ions in the presence of carboxylate-rich C-dots. Impressively, electrochemical results and X-Ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) reveal the charge storage of C-dots/CoHCFs due to two-specie (Fe and Co) redox reactions, which render an enhanced electrochemical capacity. It is considered that the activation of reversible Co(II)/Co(III) redox reaction is due to the strong coordination interaction between the terminated framework Co ions and carboxylate groups of C-dots. In addition, this nanostructure offers increased active sites and promotes electron conductivity, thereby achieving a remarkable high specific capacitance (394 F g−1 at 1 A g−1) and excellent rate performance. The C-dots/CoHCF cathode is paired with an activated carbon anode to assemble an aqueous asymmetrical supercapacitor, which can deliver a high energy density of 48.6 Wh kg–1 (488 W kg–1) and retain 88.3% of its initial capacitance over 5000 charge/discharge cycles.

head.ce:title: Coordination-induced activation of reversible Co(II)/Co(III) redox reaction in carbon nanodots/cobalt hexacyanoferrate composites with enhanced electrochemical performance

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Cobalt hexacyanoferrate (CoHCF) as a promising cathode material of asymmetrical supercapacitors generally undergoes a single redox reaction of Fe(II)/Fe(III) couple to store energy in neutral aqueous electrolytes. Its framework Co ions are electrochemically inactivated, leading to a limited electrochemical capacity. Herein, we develop a strategy to activate the reversible Co(II)/Co(III) redox reaction through tuning the microenvironment of Co ions. A carbon nanodots/CoHCF (C-dots/CoHCF) hybrid nanostructure has been successfully fabricated via a simple co-precipitation reaction of Co2+ and [Fe(CN)6]3- ions in the presence of carboxylate-rich C-dots. Impressively, electrochemical results and X-Ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) reveal the charge storage of C-dots/CoHCFs due to two-specie (Fe and Co) redox reactions, which render an enhanced electrochemical capacity. It is considered that the activation of reversible Co(II)/Co(III) redox reaction is due to the strong coordination interaction between the terminated framework Co ions and carboxylate groups of C-dots. In addition, this nanostructure offers increased active sites and promotes electron conductivity, thereby achieving a remarkable high specific capacitance (394 F g−1 at 1 A g−1) and excellent rate performance. The C-dots/CoHCF cathode is paired with an activated carbon anode to assemble an aqueous asymmetrical supercapacitor, which can deliver a high energy density of 48.6 Wh kg–1 (488 W kg–1) and retain 88.3% of its initial capacitance over 5000 charge/discharge cycles.

ce:section.ce:para: Supercapacitors with neutral aqueous electrolytes possess the remarkable features of environmental benignity, safety, inexpensive and high rate performance. Especially, an asymmetrical cell consisting of an electric double-layer capacitor (EDLC)-type anode and a battery-type cathode can provide high operation voltage (ca. more than 2.0 V), which endows aqueous supercapacitor with an additional advantage of high energy density (E = 1/2CV2, E is energy density, C is specific capacitance, V is the operating cell voltage) [1]. Tremendous successes have been achieved on the carbon-based negative electrode materials, such as activated carbon, carbon fiber, carbon nanotube, graphene, etc[2–5]. By contrast, low capacity and poor cycle stability remain critical issues for positive electrode materials. Nanostructured MnO2 or MnO2-based composites are the most studied cathode materials [6–9]. But MnO2 suffers from capacitance fading because extensive reduction of Mn4+ results in the formation of soluble Mn2+ species [10,11]. Currently, metal hexacyanoferrate (MCHF, M = Mn, Cu, Ni, Co) and its nanocomposites have attracted much attention owing to their high capacity, remarkable cycle stability and wide operation voltage window in positive potential regime [12–46].

Among MCHFs, CoHCF is regarded as the most promising cathode material for asymmetrical supercapacitors[35–46]. Its 3D open framework possessing large size channels and octahedral interstitial sites ensures that (i) rapid diffusion and sufficient accommodation of alkali metal ions are provided for energy storage; (ii) large volume variation is avoided during intercalation/deintercalation of alkali metal ions. The unique structure of CoHCF enables high rate capability and excellent cycling stability. In literatures [16,35–41], the energy storage mechanism of CoHCF has been specifically discussed that only low-spin carbon-coordinated Fe ions are electrochemically activated. Whereas, high-spin nitrogen-coordinated Co ions are electrochemically inactive in neutral aqueous electrolytes, leading to a limited capacity. Very recently, the capacity contribution of reversible Co(II)/Co(III) redox reaction has been verified on ZIF67-derived CoHCF hollow polyhedrons [42] and MnO2 nanosheets coated CoHCF [43] in Na2SO4 aqueous solution. And it has been reported that both framework Fe and Co ions can be electrochemically activated when using 4 M zinc trifluoromethanesulfonate aqueous electrolyte [47]. However, there is still a lack of understanding the features of the Co(II)/Co(III) redox reaction in neutral aqueous electrolytes. Achieving electrochemically active framework Co ions within the stable voltage window of H2O is attractive and remains challenging. Thus, it is highly desirable to develop an effective approach towards activating the reversible redox reaction of Co(II)/Co(III) couple with the goal of improving the electrochemical capacity of CoHCF.

Carbon nanodots (C-dots), a novel class of nanocarbons, have been applied in supercapacitors, lithium-ion batteries, bioimaging, sensors and photocatalytic energy conversion [48]. Owing to the high aqueous solubility, good chemical inertness and tailorable surface, C-dots are widely used to be functionalized with other materials in order to improve performance [49,50]. A variety of C-dots related nanocomposites has been fabricated with conducting polymers [51–53], porous carbon [54–56], graphene [57–59], Co(OH)2 [60], NiCo2O4 [61,62], RuO2 [63] and MnO2 [64] for applications in supercapacitors. In these studies, C-dots play roles on promoting electronic conductivity[50,60–64], accelerating ion transport [51–53], creating more active sites [50,61,64] and manipulating morphology[50,52,62]. Till date, no attempts have been made on the construction of C-dots/CoHCF hybrid nanocomposites with a synergistic effect of the two materials.

In the present work, we develop an effective approach towards activating the reversible redox reaction of Co(II)/Co(III) couple with the use of C-dots. A novel C-dots/CoHCF nanocomposite is fabricated and applied as a cathode material for high performance asymmetrical supercapacitor. During the charge/discharge processes, the valence states of framework Fe and Co ions are varied between + 2 and + 3, revealing that two-species (Fe and Co) redox reactions are involved in charge storage. The carboxylate-rich C-dots, which are capable of tuning the microenvironment of framework Co ions via strong coordination interaction, play important role on activating the reversible electrochemical redox reaction of Co(II)/Co(III) couple. Remarkable high specific capacitance is achieved for C-dots/CoHCF. Furthermore, we use the C-dots/CoHCFs material as cathode and activated carbon (AC) as anode to fabricate an asymmetrical supercapacitor (C-dots/CoHCFs//AC) cell, which exhibits high capacity as well as excellent cycling stability.

To yield C-dots/CoHCF hybrid materials, a two-step synthesis approach was utilized ( Fig. 1a). Firstly, a transparent solution of C-dots was prepared through a typical hydrothermal treatment of potassium citrate solution at 200 °C for 5 h. Then CoCl2.6H2O which was introduced into the C-dots solutions reacted with K3[Fe(CN)6] to form a C-dots/CoHCF hybrid nanostructure. As is generally known, traditional bulk CoHCF is synthesized by the co-precipitation method. The precipitation reaction of [Fe(CN)6]3- and Co2+ ions is so fast that the growth of CoHCF crystals is uncontrollable. Dark purple products of Co3[Fe(CN)6]2 are obtained immediately after mixing the solutions of CoCl2 and K3[Fe(CN)6] with the molar ratio of 3/2. The resulted CoHCF particles (CoHCF-P) display irregular morphology and severe aggregation (Fig. 1b and S1a). With the goal of fabricating CoHCF nanostructure in a controllable way, potassium citrate was employed as a complexing agent to reduce the crystal growth rates. During the synthesis process, the citrate ligands were gradually replaced by [Fe(CN)6]3- and released into solution because the coordination ability of cyanide group (CN) is stronger than that of the carboxylate (COO-) group. Ordered CoHCF nanocubes (CoHCF-NCs) were consequently obtained (Fig. 1c and S1b). Different with the nanocube morphology when using potassium citrate, uniform spherical morphology was observed for all the C-dots/CoHCF materials (Fig. 1d and S1c-e), implying that C-dots had great influences on the crystallization process of CoHCF. C-dots possess abundant surface carboxylate groups, which can coordinate with Co2+ cations. And the size of C-dots (4–8 nm) is several dozen times larger than that of citrate anion. At the initial stage, the Co2+ cations reacted with [Fe(CN)6]3- anions to form CoHCF crystal nucleus, which were anchored on the surface of C-dots. As the precipitation reaction went on, C-dots were gradually incorporated into the CoHCF spheres rather than released into solution. Though the details are still unclear, the incorporated C-dots may affect the crystallization process of CoHCF via the coordination interaction between the surface carboxylate groups and vacant Co2+ cations in the CoHCF crystal nucleus. C-dots/CoHCFs with spherical morphology were finally formed.

The crystal structure of C-dots/CoHCF is determined by XRD as illustrated in Fig. 1e and S2. The diffraction peaks of the CoHCF-P, -NC and C-dots/CoHCFs can be indexed to the face-centered cubic (FCC) structure [36–43]. Fig. 1f and S3 show the FT-IR spectra of CoHCF-P and C-dots/CoHCFs. The distinct absorption peaks at 592 and 2086 cm−1 are ascribed to the Fe-CN-Co bending mode and −CN−stretching mode, respectively [42,43]. The presence of H2O molecules in the CoHCF framework structure can be verified by the absorption peaks at around 3413 cm−1 (−OH stretching vibration) and 1611 cm−1 (H−O−H bending vibration). For C-dots/CoHCF-2 and − 3, the stretching peaks of the C–O–C (1106 cm−1) and C–H (2934, 2857 cm−1) bonds are clearly discerned [65,66]. The peak centered at 1626 cm−1 is assigned to the CO stretching vibration while the peak at 1387 cm−1 is due to COO− groups [67,68]. Taken together, the above FT-IR results confirm the existence of C-dots in the CoHCF-based nanocomposites. In Fig. 1g, the Raman characteristic peaks of CoHCF can be clearly observed. Different with CoHCF-P, C-dots/CoHCF-2 exhibits two well-defined peaks at 2092 and 2131 cm−1,[42] implying that C-dots have a significant influence on the bending vibrations (A1 g and Eg) of cyanide bond (CN). According to thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) (Fig. S4), the weight loss behaviors of C-dots/CoHCFs can be divided into three stages. They are due to the removal of water (<200 °C), decomposition of CoHCF (200 ~ 400 °C) and burning of C-dots (400–850 °C). Assuming that Co3[Fe(CN6)]2 is totally oxidized into Co3O4 and Fe2O3, the calculated contents of C-dots are approximately 8.7, 10.1 and 11.3 wt% for the C-dots/CoHCF-1, 2 and 3 samples, respectively. The N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms of the CoHCF-P and C-dots/CoHCF-2 materials are shown in Fig. S5 together with pore size distribution. C-dots/CoHCF-2 displays a type-II adsorption isotherm. The BET surface area of C-dots/CoHCF-2 is 257 m2 g−1, almost 1.74 times as high as that of CoHCF-P (148 m2 g−1).

The morphology and structure of as-prepared C-dots and C-dots/CoHCFs-2 are characterized by Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM). The spherical C-dots, which are synthesized by hydrothermally treating potassium citrate, are monodispersed with diameters of 4–8 nm ( Fig. 2a, b and S6). Fig. 2c and S7 show the TEM images of C-dots/CoHCFs-2, revealing sphere like morphology with diameters of 100–200 nm. The polycrystalline characteristic of CoHCF is indicated by the corresponding selected area electron diffraction (SEAD) (Fig. 2d). The high resolution TEM images of C-dots/CoHCFs-2 are shown in Fig. 2e. The labeled fringe distances of 0.33 and 0.53 nm can be indexed to the (002) plane of graphitic carbon [51–53] and the (200) plane of CoHCF [43], respectively. Clearly, the C-dots are dispersed inside and on the external surface of the CoHCF nanospheres. And uniform distribution of Co, Fe, N, C, O elements is verified by the energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) elemental mapping images (Fig. 2f). The surface valence states of as-prepared C-dots/CoHCFs-2 were investigated by XPS (Fig. S8a). In the Co 2p3/2 XPS binding energy spectrum ( Fig. 3a), the peaks at 781.2 and 782.9 eV are assigned to Co2+ and Co3+ ions, respectively [69,70]. Additionally satellite peaks are observed at higher XPS binding energies. The XPS binding energy spectrum of Fe 2p3/2 (Fig. 3b) is composed of two components at 708.6 and 710.0 eV, which are assigned to the Fe2+ and Fe3+ species, respectively [71,72]. It is worth noting that the Fe2+ species are dominant in the as-prepared C-dots/CoHCFs-2 although K3[Fe(CN)6] is used to react with CoCl2 in the precipitation reaction. The reason may be that the reduction of [Fe(CN)6]3- ions and the oxidation of Co2+ ions occur simultaneously during the formation of Fe-CN-Co linkage. The high-resolution O 1s XPS spectrum (Fig. S8b) can be decomposed into two components. One peak (O1) located at 531.4 eV is attributed to the CO bond, the other peak (O2) at 533.0 eV belongs to the C–OH bond. The C 1s and N 1s peaks are located at 284.9 and 398.1 eV, respectively (Fig. S8c and d).

Cyclic voltammetry (CV) measurement is performed in a three-electrode system to monitor the electrochemical redox behaviors during the charge/discharge processes. As illustrated in Fig. 4a and b, only a pair of redox peaks are observed for CoHCF-P while two pairs of well-defined redox peaks for CoHCF-NC and C-dots/CoHCFs, suggesting an obvious different electrochemical behavior between CoHCF-P and C-dots/CoHCFs (or CoHCF-NC). In addition, all the C-dots/CoHCF electrodes show significantly increased areas of CV curve in comparison with the CoHCF-P electrode. Among them, C-dots/CoHCF-2 exhibits the highest capacity. It is worth noting that no electrical double layer capacitance (EDLC) characteristic of C-dots can be identified in the CV curves of the C-dots/CoHCFs electrodes. Considering that C-dots generally possess low capacitance (<100 F g−1) [52,63] and their contents are less than 12 wt% in the nanocomposites, the capacitance contribution of C-dots can be neglected. In Fig. 4c, the oxidation (or reduction) peaks gradually shift to more positive (or negative) potentials with increasing scan rate, indicating that electrochemical redox reactions become insufficient at higher scan rate. The galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD) curves with almost symmetrical triangular shape are shown in Fig. 4d, S9 and S10a for all the electrodes. The corresponding discharge capacitances are calculated and demonstrated in Fig. S10b. For C-dots/CoHCF-2, slight increases in IR drop are observed in the range of 0.5–2 A g−1 (Fig. S11), indicating good rate performance. Its specific capacitance (394 F g−1 at 1 A g−1) is 1.83 and 1.44 times higher than that of CoHCF-P (215 F g−1) and CoHCF-NC (273 F g−1), respectively (Fig. 4e). And the calculated columbic efficiency is more than 95% (Table S1). To assess the variation of conductivity after fabricating C-dots into CoHCF, the electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) of the CoHCF-P and C-dots/CoHCFs-2 electrodes are measured. The Nyquist plots consisting of a depressed semicircle (high frequency region) and a straight line (low frequency region) are shown in Fig. 4f. In the inset of the figure, an equivalent circuit is presented for modelling the spectra (Rs is internal resistance, Rct is charge transfer resistance, ZCPE is constant phase element, and ZW is Warburg resistance). By fitting the EIS spectra, the values of Rct are determined to be 5.4 and 3.1 Ω for the CoHCF-P and C-dots/CoHCFs-2 electrodes, respectively. All the above results of CV, GCD and EIS convincingly confirm that fabricating C-dots/CoHCFs nanocomposites is effective in improving electrochemical performance. The C-dots/CoHCF electrodes provide higher charge storage ability and better electron conductivity compared to CoHCF-P.

The reaction kinetics of the C-dots/CoHCF-2 electrode are studied by means of the equation i = av
b (i is the current, v is the scan rate, a and b are variable parameters) [73,74]. The value of b parameter is used to distinguish charge storage behaviors that when b = 0.5 and 1, the electrochemical redox reaction is controlled by ionic diffusion and surface (pseudocapacitive) processes, respectively. Fig. 4g shows the scan rate dependent peak currents obtained from CV measurements (Fig. S12). The linear fits of log (i) vs. log (v) give that the slops (b values) are 0.773 and 0.767 for the cathodic and anodic peaks, respectively. This indicates that ionic diffusion and surface processes are simultaneously involved in the electrochemical redox reaction of the electrode. Both battery and pseudocapacitive behaviors are exhibited for charge storage. Furthermore, the fractions of the current belonging to ionic diffusion and surface processes at specific potentials are analyzed with the use of the equation i (V) = k 1 v + k 2 v 0.5 (k 1 and k 2 are constants).[73,74] In Fig. 4h, the capacitive contribution due to surface process is calculated to be 70% at a scan rate of 10 mV s−1 for the C-dots/CoHCF-2 electrode. At 0.5, 1, 2, 5 and 8 mV s−1, the pseudocapacitive contributions are 57%, 59%, 60%, 63% and 67%, respectively (Fig. 4i).

To understand the charge storage mechanism, the valence states of framework Co and Fe ions are characterized for the C-dots/CoHCF-2 electrode. XPS analyses show that the peak area ratios of Co2+ to Co3+ species are 0.66 and 1.42 after charging and discharging (Fig. 3a). Meanwhile, the discharged C-dots/CoHCF-2 demonstrates decreased intensity of Fe3+ species as well as increased intensity of Fe2+ species in comparison with the charged sample (Fig. 3b). These results confirm that the valences of framework Co and Fe ions are varied between + 2 and + 3 during the discharge/charge processes. Furthermore, Na+ ions which do not exist in the as–prepared C-dots/CoHCF-2 are observed in the charged and discharged samples (Fig. 3c). A comparison of Na 1 s peak intensity suggests the reversible intercalation/deintercalation of Na+ ions. In brief, the energy storage mechanism of C-dots/CoHCF-2 is interpreted that the material undergoes both Co(II)/Co(III) and Fe(II)/Fe(III) redox reactions to store/release charges. Meanwhile, Na+ ions are intercalated/deintercalated into/out the 3D open framework of CoHCF for maintaining charge neutrality.

It is known that the thermodynamic stable potential window of H2O is 1.23 V, which limits not only the operation cell voltage of aqueous supercapacitors but also the electrochemical capacity of electrode materials. In relation to CoHCF, a restriction on the Co(II)/Co(III) redox reaction is imposed by the low electrochemical potential of water oxidation.[16] Only framework Fe ions are electrochemically active within the stable potential window of H2O and their valences are reversibly varied between + 2 and + 3 for charge storage/release.[16,35–41] This one specie (Fe) redox reaction mechanism is also applicable to the energy storage of the prepared CoHCF-P which exhibits one pair of redox peaks in the CV. Whereas for C-dots/CoHCFs, the XPS results and CV curves evidently point to that both Fe and Co ions are electrochemically active for charge storage in the potential range of 0.0–1.0 V (vs. SCE). The activation of Co(II)/Co(III) redox reaction can reasonably be attributed to the designed nanostructure in which C-dots exert a remarkable effect on the framework Co ions.

CoHCF is a typical coordination complex that Co and Fe ions are alternately six-fold coordinated with the nitrogen and carbon atoms of the cyanide ligand (–CN–). In general, considerable Fe(CN)6 vacancies are formed in the CoHCF framework during the precipitation reaction of Co2+ cations and [Fe(CN)6]3- anions. Different from the Co ions octahedrally coordinated by six nitrogen atoms of CN, the Co ions at the Fe(CN)6 vacancy sites (Co(V)) are in unsaturated coordination environments. In CoHCF-P, the Co(V) ions are coordinated by H2O molecules,[12,13] exhibiting no capacity contribution from the Co(II)/Co(III) redox couple (Fig. 4a). Whereas for C-dots/CoHCF (Fig. 3d), it is reasonably speculated that these Co(V) cations coordinate with carboxylate groups of C-dots instead of H2O molecules. The strong coordination interaction can tailor the electronic structure of Fe−CN−Co bond and lower the redox potential of Co species. As a result, the activation of reversible Co(II)/Co(III) redox reaction is achieved within the stable potential window of H2O. As illustrated in Fig. 4b, the peaks located at 0.93 V (oxidation) and 0.82 V (reduction) are attributed to the Co(II)/Co(III) redox couple while the peaks at 0.51 and 0.33 V are due to the Fe(II)/Fe(III) redox couple. Significant enhancement in electrochemical capacity is consequently achieved because of the two-species redox reactions of Fe(II)/Fe(III) and Co(II)/Co(III) couples. Furthermore, it is worth noting that C-dots/CoHCFs exhibit much higher peak intensity of Co(II)/Co(III) redox reaction than CoHCF-NC. The reason is that in CoHCF-NC, citrate groups adhered on the outer surface can interact with only surface Co cations (Fig. 3e). Whereas for C-dots/CoHCF, the coordination interaction between the Co(V) cations and carboxylate groups of C-dots take places not only on the outer surface but also within the nanocomposites, suppling more active sites for energy storage. More active framework Co(V) cations participating in the charge storage/release reactions, higher capacitances are achieved.

The specific capacitance of C-dots/CoHCF is summarized in Table 1, showing the highest value among the reported CoHCF-based electrode materials. At least 183% improvement in capacitance is achieved on C-dots/CoHCF-2 in comparison with CoHCF-P. The high specific capacitance of C-dots/CoHCFs can be mainly attributed to the Faradaic redox reactions related with Fe(II)/Fe(III) and Co(II)/Co(III) couples on basis of the above-mentioned analyses on the CV curves and XPS results. Thanks to the strong coordination interaction of carboxylate groups of C-dots, the framework Co cations at the Fe(CN)6 vacancy sites can be electrochemically activated within the thermodynamic stable potential window of H2O, enhancing electrochemical capacity. Besides, the C-dots play important and unique roles on fabricating nanostructure and improving rate performance. The effects are addressed as follows: (1) the C-dots inhibit the aggregation of CoHCF nanoparticles during the crystallization process, and thereby the C-dots/CoHCF nanospheres with smaller size can provide higher surface areas for sodium ion storage. (2) the C-dots, which are dispersed inside and on the external surface of CoHCF spheres, serve as conductive regions to promote electron transfer inside one particle and between particles. (3) the C-dots hybridized 3D structure is beneficial for sodium ion diffusion. Briefly, this C-dots/CoHCF hybrid nanostructure induces the activation of Co(II)/Co(III) redox reaction within the stable potential window of H2O, provides more active sites for electrochemical reactions, prevents the aggregation of CoHCF nanospheres, enhances electron and ion transport during the charge/discharge cycling.

Encouragingly, the C-dots/CoHCF material exhibits high specific capacitance and excellent rate capability in the positive potential regime, thus suggesting a promising cathode material for aqueous asymmetrical supercapacitor. A C-dots/CoHCF//AC hybrid supercapacitor is constructed with using commercial AC as anode. The working mechanism of C-dots/CoHCF//AC is sketched in Fig. 5a. Na+ ions are deintercalated out of the C-dots/CoHCF cathode and absorbed into the AC anode during charging, and the reverse process occurs during discharging. Fig. 5b presents the stable potential windows of the AC anode and C-dots/CoHCF-2 cathode, which are in the range of −1.0–0.0 and 0–1.1 V, respectively. The different working potential windows ensure that the cell voltage of C-dots/CoHCF//AC can be expanded up to 2.1 V. In Fig. 5c and d, the CV curves recorded at different cell voltages (1.8–2.1 V) and scan rates (5–50 mV s−1) demonstrate typical faradaic redox behavior of the as-assembled C-dots/CoHCF//AC hybrid capacitor. Fig. 5e shows highly symmetrical GCD curves, exhibiting good electrochemical reversibility. In Fig. 5f, the rate performance of the device is investigated that the discharge capacitances are 91.3, 87.7, 83.2, and 71.5 F g−1 at 0.2, 0.5, 1, 3 A g−1, respectively. The Ragone plot of C-dots/CoHCF//AC (Fig. 5g) demonstrates that its energy density reaches 48. 9 Wh kg−1 at a power density of 488 W kg−1 and still retains 42.8 Wh kg−1 at 2180 W kg−1. The performance of our hybrid capacitor is superior or comparable to most CoHCF-based asymmetrical supercapacitors (Table S2), such as CoHCF//carbon black modified graphene (34.4 Wh kg−1 at 2.5 kW kg−1) [37], CoHCF//carbon micro-spheres (54.4 Wh kg−1 at 800 W kg−1) [38], CoHCF hollow prisms//AC (47 Wh kg−1 at 1000 W kg−1) [39], CoHCF submicroboxes//AC (42.5 Wh kg−1 at 990 W kg−1),[40] CoHCF/reduced graphene oxide//AC (39.6 Wh kg−1 at 1.0 kW kg−1) [41], ZIF-67 derived CoHCF//AC (50 Wh kg−1 at 1000 W kg−1) [42] and CoHCF/MnO2//AC (37.6 Wh kg−1 at 1.1 kW kg−1) [43]. It should be noted that despite high capacitance and excellent rate capability of the C-dots/CoHCF cathode, the performance of C-dots/CoHCF//AC is significantly limited by the commercial AC anode. Furthermore, long-term cycling tests of the hybrid capacitor cell are performed that the capacitance retention is 88.3% (1 A g−1) over 5000 charge-discharge cycles (Fig. 5h). The as-assembled C-dots/CoHCF//AC asymmetrical supercapacitor can offer a comparable energy density as well as cycling stability to Pb-acid battery, demonstrating its great potential for energy storage systems.

In summary, novel C-dots/CoHCF nanocomposites have been fabricated as positive electrodes for neutral aqueous asymmetrical supercapacitors. This C-dots/CoHCF hybrid nanostructure is conducive to the activation of the Co(II)/Co(III) redox reaction, which can be attributed to the strong coordination interaction between the framework Co ions and carboxylate groups of C-dots. The incorporation of conductive C-dots also promotes electron transfers inside a particle and between particles. Owing to the two-species (Fe and Co) redox reaction mechanism and enhanced electronic conductivity, the C-dots/CoHCF nanocomposite exhibits a remarkable high specific capacitance of 394 F g−1 at 1 A g−1 and excellent rate performance. Furthermore, a high performance asymmetrical supercapacitor cell is assembled with using C-dots/CoHCF as cathode and AC as anode. This work provides a deep insight into the charge storage mechanism of CoHCF. And the approach that employs C-dots to fabricate high performance hybrid electrode materials may be applicable to CuFe Prussian blue analogues.

Zhaoxia Song: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft preparation. Wei Liu: Supervision, Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing. Quan Zhou: Investigation, Data curation. Jiaqi Du: Investigation, Data curation, Visualization. Junlin Chen: Investigation, Data curation, Visualization. Xiaofei Wei: Formal analysis. Ruinian Hua: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing. Zhongfu Zhao: Methodology, Writing – review & editing.
